The value of T1 is `>:Int` and the value of T2 is `==Union{}` with additional constraint `T1 >: T2`. This is fairly trivial to find value for `T1` and `T2` that satisfy those relations. The `Test.detect_unbound_args` code even correctly identifies this as true:
> 
> ```
> julia> Tuple{Int, Vector{Union{}}} <: Tuple{T, Vector{<:T}} where {T}
> true
> 
> julia> foo(1, [1.1])
> ERROR: UndefVarError: `T1` not defined in static parameter matching
> Suggestion: run Test.detect_unbound_args to detect method arguments that do not fully constrain a type parameter.
> ```

Currently, in Julia 1.11.1, `foo(1, [1.1])` does not throw any error, which is the issue I was referring to. If this is already fixed on the "master" branch, please ignore my comment. You only have one UnionAll variable, so it should only accept one application, though if that application is a UnionAll variable, then the result can accept another one again

How is it acceptable that a more specialized version of `MyDict` is not even the subtype of `MyDict`?
```
julia> (MyDict{T, Dict{Vector{Int}, Array{T, 2}}} where {T}) <: MyDict
false
```

Moreover, `MyDict{T, Dict{Vector{Int}, Array{T, 2}}} where {T} <: C` does not even meet its bound `AbstractDict{<:Array, <:Array{T}} where {T}`:
```
julia> bound = AbstractDict{<:Array, <:Array{T}} where {T}
AbstractDict{<:Array, <:Array{T}} where T

julia> (MyDict{T, Dict{Vector{Int}, Array{T, 2}}} where {T}) <: bound
false
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2502508526/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

1. 
```Julia
julia> const TypeofMyT{T, C<:Vector{T}} = Type{C}
Type{C} where {T, C<:Vector{T}}

julia> TypeofMyT{Integer, <:Vector{<:Real}}
Type{<:Vector{<:Real}}
```
which  should have been `TypeofMyT{Integer, <:Vector{Integer}}` (or should not have been allowed, which is debateble) because
```Julia
julia> Vector{Integer} <: Vector{<:Real}
true

julia> Vector{<:Real} <: Vector{Integer}
false

julia> TypeofMyT{Integer, <:Vector{<:Real}} <: TypeofMyT
false
```

2.
```Julia
julia> const MyT{T, C<:Vector{T}} = C
Vector (alias for Array{T, 1} where T)
```
It is already concerning that the compiler automatically converts `MyT` back to `vector`. Consequently, it does not even allow a specification of the second parameter:
```Julia
julia> MyT{<:Any, Vector{Float64}}
ERROR: too many parameters for type Array
```
Just because there's a reason for such behavior does not automatically justify it. If you allow the user to rename a type legally with additional type parameters, you should support full feature and consistent type inference for all of these parameters or forbid such construction altogether. Calling this behavior "correct," IMHO, does not seem a convincing solution.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2502560530/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

If you have an abstract type with a constrained type parameter, subtypes whose parameters inherit from the type's parameters do not inherit the constraints.
```julia
abstract type AbstractFoo{T<:Real}
end

struct Foo{T} <: AbstractFoo{T} end
```

```julia-repl
julia> supertype(Foo)
AbstractFoo{T} where T

julia> supertype(Foo{<:Real}}
AbstractFoo

julia> Foo <: AbstractFoo    # This one is very much unexpected!
false

julia> Foo{<:Real} <: AbstractFoo
true
```
This is true even though you can't construct a type which violates the constraints on its parent's parameters:
```julia-repl
julia> Foo{String}
ERROR: TypeError: in AbstractFoo, in T, expected T<:Real, got Type{String}
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[5]:1
```
This is a subtle point that is not included in the documentation on abstract parametric types, but has caused me some headaches when some subtype relations broke unexpectedly.

The above examples were tested on Julia 1.10 (version info below) running on Arch Linux in WSL2, installed with the AUR provided `julia-bin` package that provides the official release:
```julia-repl
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.10.0

  Threads: 1 on 20 virtual cores
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53380/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":2,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53380/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2240002609","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53380#issuecomment-2240002609","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53380","id":2240002609,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6Fg7ox","user":{"login":"frankwswang","id":41162655,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQxMTYyNjU1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/41162655?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/frankwswang","html_url":"https://github.com/frankwswang","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/frankwswang/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/frankwswang/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/frankwswang/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/frankwswang/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/frankwswang/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/frankwswang/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/frankwswang/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/frankwswang/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/frankwswang/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-07-19T19:39:08Z","updated_at":"2024-07-19T19:39:08Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"I want to add a relevant example simplified from another [discourse post](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/best-practices-to-add-more-than-one-bound-to-type-parameters/117212/2?u=frankwswang), which I think also behaves like a bug. ```julia
julia> t = Array{T, N} where {N, T0<:Union{Integer, Float64}, NTuple{N, T0}<:T<:Union{Tuple{Real}, Tuple{Real, Real}}}
Array{T, N} where {N, T0<:Union{Float64, Integer}, NTuple{N, T0}<:T<:Union{Tuple{Real}, Tuple{Real, Real}}}

julia> [(1,2);;;] isa t
false
```
Apparently, `N` in `NTuple{N, T0}` is registered by the compiler; otherwise, `[(1,2);;;] isa t` would return `true`. We can verify this by defining and checking an alternative type `t2`:
```julia
julia> t2 = Array{T, N} where {N, T<:Union{Tuple{Real}, Tuple{Real, Real}}}
Array{T, N} where {N, T<:Union{Tuple{Real}, Tuple{Real, Real}}}

julia> [(1,2);;;] isa t2
true
```
However, when we test the following:
```julia
julia> [(Float32(1.0),)] isa t
true
```
The constraint on `T0<:Union{Float64, Integer}` is omitted even though `NTuple{N, T0}<:T<:Union{Tuple{Real}, Tuple{Real, Real}}`  is permitted in the first place.

Is this mismatch considered a "bug" or a "feature" of Julia's type system? mentioned in a [Discourse post](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/best-practices-to-add-more-than-one-bound-to-type-parameters/117212/8?u=frankwswang):
```julia
julia> t = Array{T, N} where {N, T0<:Union{Integer, Float64}, NTuple{N, T0}<:T<:Union{Tuple{Real}, Tuple{Real, Real}}}
Array{T, N} where {N, T0<:Union{Float64, Integer}, NTuple{N, T0}<:T<:Union{Tuple{Real}, Tuple{Real, Real}}}

julia> [(1,2);;;] isa t
false
```
Apparently, `N` in `NTuple{N, T0}` is registered by the compiler; otherwise, `[(1,2);;;] isa t` would return `true`. We can verify this by defining and checking an alternative type `t2`:
```julia
julia> t2 = Array{T, N} where {N, T<:Union{Tuple{Real}, Tuple{Real, Real}}}
Array{T, N} where {N, T<:Union{Tuple{Real}, Tuple{Real, Real}}}

julia> [(1,2);;;] isa t2
true
```
However, when we test the following:
```julia
julia> [(Float32(1.0),)] isa t
true
```
The constraint on `T0<:Union{Float64, Integer}` is omitted even though `NTuple{N, T0}<:T<:Union{Tuple{Real}, Tuple{Real, Real}}`  is permitted in the first place.

Is this mismatch considered a "bug" or a "feature" of Julia's type system? arguments","user":{"login":"Liozou","id":23532352,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzNTMyMzUy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23532352?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Liozou","html_url":"https://github.com/Liozou","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Liozou/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Liozou/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Liozou/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Liozou/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Liozou/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Liozou/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Liozou/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Liozou/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Liozou/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2022-03-03T22:53:53Z","updated_at":"2022-04-02T14:17:47Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/44434","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44434","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44434.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44434.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"When defining a function with both optional and keyword arguments, the representation of the resulting methods do not show the keyword arguments, except for the last one. For example, there is no `foo(a; kwarg)` method appearing in the following list, although calling `foo(3; kwarg=6)` is valid:
```julia
julia> foo(a, b=3; kwarg) = nothing
foo (generic function with 2 methods)

julia> methods(foo)
# 2 methods for generic function "foo":
[1] foo(a) in Main at REPL[1]:1
[2] foo(a, b; kwarg) in Main at REPL[1]:1
```

I suppose this comes from the actual implementation of method dispatch with keyword arguments, with the kwsorter and stuff, and there are good reasons why those methods are defined like that. Without changing any of these internals thus, this PR changes the **representation** of the `MethodList` created when calling `methods(foo)`, in order to show a "method" closer to the definition:
```julia
julia> methods(foo)
# 2 methods for generic function "foo":
[1-2] foo(a, [b]; kwarg) in Main at REPL[1]:1
```

This is done by converting the `MethodList` to the new `FactoredMethodList` type upon calling `show`, so the `MethodList` itself is untouched. You can still access the previous representation by looking at the actual list of methods of course:
```julia
julia> methods(foo).ms
[1] foo(a) in Main at REPL[1]:1
[2] foo(a, b; kwarg) in Main at REPL[1]:1
```

The detection of method definitions corresponding to the same line of definition in code except for the presence of optional arguments works by comparing the locations of the method definitions as well as their argument names and types. It's not perfect since you could define methods at the same position through macros or `@eval`, but I don't think it's too bad since there is no loss of information anyway, all previously represented methods will still be represented (in a more compact way possibly).

This is also ported to REPL completions, which was the initial reason for this PR (it solves an issue that blocks #43536 at the moment):
```julia
julia> iterate(trues(6), #TAB
iterate(B::BitArray, [i::Int64]) in Base at bitarray.jl:361
iterate(A::AbstractArray, state) in Base at abstractarray.jl:1159
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44434/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":1,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44434/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1086650308","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44434#issuecomment-1086650308","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44434","id":1086650308,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5AxPfE","user":{"login":"frankwswang","id":41162655,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQxMTYyNjU1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/41162655?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/frankwswang","html_url":"https://github.com/frankwswang","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/frankwswang/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/frankwswang/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/frankwswang/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/frankwswang/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/frankwswang/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/frankwswang/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/frankwswang/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/frankwswang/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/frankwswang/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-04-02T14:17:47Z","updated_at":"2022-04-02T14:17:47Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"The current representation can lead to inaccurate exception info in some cases like this:

Thanks for the reply! Your reason is exactly what I wanted to question. Why should `myT{A}(args...)` necessarily correspond to `myT{A, B}`? It's a constructor, hence just a `Function` method that creates the actual `struct` of type `myT` with certain type parameters. You can even define a method (as an outer constructor) like
```
myT{T}() where {T} = T
```
which doesn't actually construct a new instance of `myT`. So I would argue `myT{A}(args...)` is closer to a generic function than an extension of the parametric type. Also, if each item in `(args...)` doesn't have to strictly correspond to each field of the created type instance, why should the parameters in `{T...}` of the constructor do? The function and the type are one and the same. Whether it constructs an instance or not is not relevant; what matters is that the type itself is the called object. So I believe this is not really possible to implement.
> 
> To elaborate further, `myT{A}(x)` does the same thing as
> ```
> f = myT{A}
> f(x)
> ```
> so the `myT{A}` syntax indeed must mean the same thing in both cases.

I guess this really unveiled the different perspectives of treating `f` here. Is it a function that looks like type `myT` or a type instance that can be treated like a function?
